skill.name: state-management
skill.description: Implement state management patterns for frontend applications. Use when managing global state, handling complex data flows, or coordinating state across components. Handles React Context, Redux, Zustand, Recoil, and state management best practices.
skill.summary: Implement state management patterns for frontend applications. Use when managing global state, handling complex data flows, or coordinating state across components.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 69 potential security issues, all of which are false positives from markdown code blocks and example code. The skill contains legitimate educational content about React state management patterns (Context, Redux, Zustand, Recoil). Detected patterns include backticks in markdown fence blocks (not Ruby execution), fetch calls in example code (demonstrating proper API patterns), relative markdown links (not path traversal), and localStorage usage (standard frontend authentication patterns). No executable code or security risks present.

content.user_title: Implement State Management in React Apps
content.value_statement: Struggling with props drilling and scattered state across components? This skill provides proven patterns for managing global state in React applications using Context, Redux, Zustand, and React Query.
actual_capabilities: Determine when to use local vs global state based on component hierarchy
Implement React Context API for simple global state like authentication and themes
Build Zustand stores for medium-complexity state like shopping carts and user settings
Create Redux Toolkit slices for large-scale applications with complex state logic
Implement React Query for server state management with automatic caching and refetching
limitations: Only covers React ecosystem state management solutions
Does not address Angular, Vue, or Svelte state management patterns
Focused on client state with limited coverage of server state synchronization
Examples use TypeScript and may require adaptation for JavaScript projects

use_cases.title: E-commerce Shopping Cart
use_cases.description: Implement a persistent shopping cart that maintains state across product pages, cart modal, and checkout flow. Track items, quantities, and totals with automatic localStorage persistence.

use_cases.title: User Authentication System
use_cases.description: Create global authentication state accessible across navigation, profile, and protected routes. Manage user data, login/logout actions, and token storage with Context API.

use_cases.title: Real-time Dashboard with Server Data
use_cases.description: Build a dashboard that fetches and caches server data using React Query. Handle loading states, automatic refetching, and optimistic updates for admin interfaces.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Right State Management Tool
prompt_templates.prompt: I am building a [app type] with [state complexity]. The state includes [examples of state data]. Which state management solution should I use and why?
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner developers unsure which tool to choose for their project

prompt_templates.title: Implement React Context for Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a React Context provider for user authentication. Include user state, login/logout functions, token storage in localStorage, and a custom useAuth hook with error handling.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developers implementing simple authentication state across components

prompt_templates.title: Build Zustand Store for Shopping Cart
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Zustand store for a shopping cart with items array, add/remove/update quantity actions, total calculation, and devtools + persist middleware for localStorage.

prompt_templates.title: Redux Toolkit with Async Data Fetching
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up Redux Toolkit with createAsyncThunk for fetching [data type] from an API. Include the slice with pending/fulfilled/rejected handlers, typed hooks, and the store configuration.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced developers managing complex state with server synchronization

output_examples.input: I need to manage user authentication state across my React app. Components in Header, Profile, and Settings need access to user data.
output_examples.output: Based on your requirements, React Context API is the best fit. Here is a complete AuthContext implementation with login/logout functions, localStorage token persistence, and a useAuth custom hook that provides user data and authentication status to all components.

output_examples.input: I am building an e-commerce site and need a shopping cart that persists across page navigations. Users can add items, change quantities, and remove products.
output_examples.output: For this e-commerce shopping cart, Zustand is ideal. It provides less boilerplate than Redux while handling medium-complexity state perfectly. Here is a cart store with add/remove/update actions, total calculation, and persist middleware that automatically saves cart data to localStorage so items persist across sessions.

output_examples.input: My dashboard displays user data from our API. I need to fetch data, show loading states, handle errors, and cache responses to avoid unnecessary requests.
output_examples.output: React Query (TanStack Query) is designed exactly for this server state scenario. It handles caching, background refetching, and loading/error states automatically. Here is a UserProfile component using useQuery for data fetching and useMutation for updates with automatic cache invalidation.

best_practices: Subscribe only to the specific state slices you need rather than the entire store to prevent unnecessary re-renders
Keep state local until multiple components need access, then promote to global state to avoid over-engineering simple component state
Use TypeScript interfaces to type your state, actions, and store definitions for better IDE support and runtime safety
anti_patterns: Storing derived values in state when they can be calculated from existing state, such as storing item count separately from the items array
Making everything global state when local component state would suffice, which adds unnecessary complexity and reduces performance
Subscribing to entire stores or contexts when you only need specific values, causing components to re-render on unrelated state changes

faq.question: When should I use Context API versus Redux or Zustand?
faq.answer: Use Context API for simple global state like themes, authentication, or language settings. Choose Zustand for medium complexity like shopping carts or user settings. Use Redux Toolkit for large-scale applications with complex state logic, middleware requirements, or when you need advanced devtools and time-travel debugging.

faq.question: Should I use React Query for all API calls?
faq.answer: React Query excels at server state management with caching, refetching, and synchronization. Use it for data that comes from APIs and can become stale. For client-only state like form inputs, UI toggles, or modal states, use local component state, Context, or Zustand instead.

faq.question: Why am I getting too many re-renders with my state management?
faq.answer: This commonly occurs when subscribing to more state than needed. With Zustand, use selector functions: const items = useCartStore(state => state.items). With Redux, use specific selectors: const todos = useSelector(state => state.todos.items). Avoid subscribing to entire stores when you only need specific values.

faq.question: Can I use multiple state management solutions together?
faq.answer: Yes, and this is often the best approach. Use React Query for server data, Zustand for client state like shopping carts, and local useState for component-specific state. The key is using each tool for its strengths rather than trying to force everything into one solution.

faq.question: How do I handle forms with state management?
faq.answer: For most forms, use React Hook Form combined with Zod for validation. This keeps form state local and optimized. Only integrate with global state management when the form data affects other components or needs to persist across routes, such as a multi-step wizard or user settings form.

faq.question: Is Redux still relevant with newer options available?
faq.answer: Yes, Redux Toolkit remains the best choice for large-scale applications with complex state logic, middleware requirements (like logging, persistence, or crash reporting), and teams that benefit from structured state patterns. However, for smaller apps, Zustand or Context API provide simpler alternatives with less boilerplate.
